note.soap:
S: 35-year-old patient. The patient reports 6 days of sinus pressure and congestion with clear nasal discharge. The symptoms have been staying the same without improvement. There is facial pressure present, no fever, and features suggestive of allergies. The patient denies any vision changes or severe neurological symptoms. Patient is not pregnant.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No fever reported. No signs of severe infection or complications noted based on history. No red flags for vision or neurological involvement.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Allergic rhinosinusitis with possible viral sinus congestion  
  - Differential: Viral sinusitis, bacterial sinusitis (less likely without fever or purulent discharge), allergic rhinitis  
  - Red flags considered: Vision changes, severe neurological symptoms, high fever, worsening symptoms—none present

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage conservatively at home with close follow-up in primary care or urgent care if symptoms worsen or fail to improve.  
  - Plan: Recommend saline nasal irrigation and oral antihistamines for allergy symptoms. Advise symptomatic treatment with analgesics as needed for facial pressure. No antibiotics indicated at this time given lack of fever and purulent discharge.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care if fever develops, facial pain worsens, nasal discharge becomes purulent or bloody, vision changes occur, or neurological symptoms develop.

Evidence (snippets used):  
Duration 6 days, symptoms staying the same, clear discharge, facial pressure present, no fever, allergy features present, no eye or severe neuro red flags, pregnancy status negative.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
